did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for the detection and classification of skin diseases. Specifically, it proposes a CNN architecture with nine convolution layers and two fully connected layers to identify ten distinct skin diseases, achieving an overall accuracy of 91.07%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a specific Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture with nine convolution layers and two fully connected layers for the detection of ten distinct skin diseases. It reports original results, including an overall accuracy of 91.07%, and describes the methodology, dataset usage, and classification process, which characterizes it as a research study rather than a review.

title: Convolutional Neural Networks for The Detection of Skin Diseases
journal: 2023 International Conference on Emerging Research in Computational Science (ICERCS)
brief_summary: This study proposes a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture with nine convolution layers and two fully connected layers to detect and classify ten distinct skin diseases. The model was evaluated using the Dermnet dataset, achieving an overall accuracy of 91.07%. Additionally, the paper discusses the use of five different CNN architectures, including Inception ResNet v2, for diagnosing psoriasis and eczema, with the latter achieving testing accuracies of 97.1% and 95.8% respectively. The research highlights the potential of deep learning to provide accurate, real-time diagnosis of dermatological conditions.

lesion_types_studied.details: Mentioned as a primary target for detection and classification in the context of skin cancer diagnosis. The paper notes it is the most common diagnosis in resource-rich regions and the most dangerous kind of skin cancer.

The paper proposes a CNN to detect 'ten distinct skin diseases' with 91.07% accuracy using the Dermnet dataset. It also describes a prototype classifier handling 'five types of skin disorders' and using 500 photos from the Dermnet dataset depicting 'various illnesses'.

all_task_types.details: The paper proposes a CNN architecture with nine convolution layers and two fully connected layers to classify ten distinct skin diseases, achieving an overall accuracy of 91.07%. It also classifies five specific skin diseases (lichen simplex, eczema hand, eczema nummular, eczema sub cute, stasis dermatitis, and ulcers) and diagnoses psoriasis and eczema using five different CNN architectures (including Inception ResNet v2), achieving testing accuracies of 97.1% and 95.8% respectively.

datasets_used.name: Dermnet dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing images of various skin diseases. The study utilized a subset of 500 photos from this dataset depicting various illnesses for a prototype classifier.

use.training: Used for training a prototype classifier to identify five types of skin disorders.
use.testing: Used for testing the prototype classifier, which achieved 73% accuracy.

datasets_used.description: A subset of the Dermnet dataset used to train and evaluate a proposed CNN architecture with nine convolution layers and two fully connected layers for detecting ten distinct skin diseases.

use.testing: Used to evaluate the model, achieving an overall accuracy of 91.07%.

datasets_used.description: A dataset used to diagnose psoriasis and eczema using five different CNN architectures, including Inception ResNet v2. The specific source is not named beyond the condition types, but it is distinct from the Dermnet subset used for the 5-class and 10-class experiments.

use.training: Used to train five different CNN architectures using fine-tuning with Adam and Rmsprop optimizers.
use.validation: Used to validate the models, with Inception ResNet v2 achieving 97.1% validation accuracy.
use.testing: Used to test the models, with Inception ResNet v2 achieving 95.8% testing accuracy.

preprocessing_techniques: Skin photographs are filtered to remove unnecessary noise and processed to enhance image quality.
Preprocessing is used to remove various artifacts from raw photos.
Active Contour segmentation technique is used to extract the region of interest (segmentation masks).
k-means clustering is used to segment dermoscopic skin lesion images to determine the region of interest for the lesion component.
conclusions: The proposed Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture, consisting of nine convolution layers and two fully connected layers, achieved an overall accuracy of 91.07% in detecting and classifying ten distinct skin diseases.
The proposed CNN paradigm efficiently addresses issues related to the diagnosis and classification of skin disorders and appears promising compared to earlier methods.

For the diagnosis of psoriasis and eczema, the Inception ResNet v2 architecture outperformed four other CNN architectures, achieving validation and testing accuracies of 97.1% and 95.8%, respectively.

The study demonstrates two practical implementation strategies for skin disease diagnosis: a smartphone-centric approach and a web server-centric approach, enabling real-time diagnosis and severity evaluation.
Deep learning-based methods using CNNs provide computational support that can help medical science progress in the detection of skin diseases.
important_remarks: The study relies on a very small prototype dataset of only 500 photos from the Dermnet dataset for the initial classifier, which limits statistical power and generalizability.
The paper reports an overall accuracy of 91.07% for the 10-class CNN but does not provide a breakdown of per-class performance, making it impossible to assess performance on specific or rare classes.
No information is provided regarding the train/validation/test split sizes or ratios for the main 10-class experiment, raising concerns about potential data leakage or overfitting.
The paper does not report data augmentation techniques, which is a significant omission for deep learning models trained on relatively small medical imaging datasets.
The source of the dataset used for the Psoriasis and Eczema diagnosis (Inception ResNet v2) is not explicitly named, only described as a 'dataset for psoriasis and eczema', hindering reproducibility.

The paper mixes results from different experimental setups (prototype classifier, 10-class CNN, and comparative study of KNN/DTC/LBP) without clearly delineating whether they are on the same or different data subsets.
The comparative study of KNN, DTC, and LBP lacks context regarding the dataset size and preprocessing steps specific to that experiment, making direct comparison with the CNN results difficult.
The paper claims the system is suitable for 'real-time diagnosis' and 'emergency medical care' but provides no latency or inference time metrics to support these deployment claims.
There is no mention of external validation on independent datasets, which is critical for assessing the clinical relevance and robustness of the proposed CNN architecture.
The paper does not discuss potential biases in the Dermnet dataset (e.g., skin tone diversity, image quality variations) which could affect model fairness and performance in diverse populations.
